data.title: Atlas Copco AB (publ) (STO:ATCO A) Just Released Its First-Quarter Results And Analysts Are Updating Their Estimates
data.description:
Atlas Copco AB (publ) (STO:ATCO A) shareholders are probably feeling a little disappointed, since its shares fell 6.8% to kr174 in the week after its latest quarterly results. It looks like the results were a bit of a negative overall. While revenues of kr41b were in line with analyst predictions, statutory earnings were less than expected, missing estimates by 4.1% to hit kr1.28 per share. The analysts typically update their forecasts at each earnings report, and we can judge from their estimates whether their view of the company has changed or if there are any new concerns to be aware of. So we gathered the latest post-earnings forecasts to see what estimates suggest is in store for next year.

This technology could replace computers: discover the 20 stocks are working to make quantum computing a reality.

After the latest results, the 19 analysts covering Atlas Copco are now predicting revenues of kr174.4b in 2026. If met, this would reflect a modest 5.0% improvement in revenue compared to the last 12 months. Statutory earnings per share are predicted to rise 8.1% to kr5.78. Yet prior to the latest earnings, the analysts had been anticipated revenues of kr175.5b and earnings per share (EPS) of kr5.89 in 2026. So it's pretty clear that, although the analysts have updated their estimates, there's been no major change in expectations for the business following the latest results.

Check out our latest analysis for Atlas Copco

The analysts reconfirmed their price target of kr195, showing that the business is executing well and in line with expectations. That's not the only conclusion we can draw from this data however, as some investors also like to consider the spread in estimates when evaluating analyst price targets. Currently, the most bullish analyst values Atlas Copco at kr220 per share, while the most bearish prices it at kr145. As you can see, analysts are not all in agreement on the stock's future, but the range of estimates is still reasonably narrow, which could suggest that the outcome is not totally unpredictable.

These estimates are interesting, but it can be useful to paint some more broad strokes when seeing how forecasts compare, both to the Atlas Copco's past performance and to peers in the same industry. We would highlight that Atlas Copco's revenue growth is expected to slow, with the forecast 6.7% annualised growth rate until the end of 2026 being well below the historical 11% p.a. growth over the last five years. Juxtapose this against the other companies in the industry with analyst coverage, which are forecast to grow their revenues (in aggregate) 6.6% annually. So it's pretty clear that, while Atlas Copco's revenue growth is expected to slow, it's expected to grow roughly in line with the industry.

Advertisement

The Bottom Line

The most important thing to take away is that there's been no major change in sentiment, with the analysts reconfirming that the business is performing in line with their previous earnings per share estimates. They also reconfirmed their revenue estimates, with the company predicted to grow at about the same rate as the wider industry. There was no real change to the consensus price target, suggesting that the intrinsic value of the business has not undergone any major changes with the latest estimates.

With that in mind, we wouldn't be too quick to come to a conclusion on Atlas Copco. Long-term earnings power is much more important than next year's profits. We have forecasts for Atlas Copco going out to 2028, and you can see them free on our platform here.

You can also see whether Atlas Copco is carrying too much debt, and whether its balance sheet is healthy, for free on our platform here.

data.title: YOC AG Reported A Surprise Loss, And Analysts Have Updated Their Forecasts
data.description:
It's been a good week for YOC AG (ETR:YOC) shareholders, because the company has just released its latest annual results, and the shares gained 3.6% to €6.88. Revenues of €39m beat expectations by 2.1%. Unfortunately statutory earnings per share (EPS) fell well short of the mark, turning in a loss of €0.12 compared to previous analyst expectations of a profit. This is an important time for investors, as they can track a company's performance in its report, look at what experts are forecasting for next year, and see if there has been any change to expectations for the business. So we collected the latest post-earnings statutory consensus estimates to see what could be in store for next year.

Trump has pledged to "unleash" American oil and gas and these 15 US stocks have developments that are poised to benefit.

Following the latest results, YOC's two analysts are now forecasting revenues of €40.6m in 2026. This would be a modest 4.5% improvement in revenue compared to the last 12 months. YOC is also expected to turn profitable, with statutory earnings of €0.61 per share. Before this earnings report, the analysts had been forecasting revenues of €42.0m and earnings per share (EPS) of €0.62 in 2026. The consensus seems maybe a little more pessimistic, trimming their revenue forecasts after the latest results even though there was no change to its EPS estimates.

View our latest analysis for YOC

The average price target was reduced 20% to €18.50, with the lower revenue forecasts indicating negative sentiment towards YOC, even though earnings forecasts were unchanged.

One way to get more context on these forecasts is to look at how they compare to both past performance, and how other companies in the same industry are performing. It's pretty clear that there is an expectation that YOC's revenue growth will slow down substantially, with revenues to the end of 2026 expected to display 4.5% growth on an annualised basis. This is compared to a historical growth rate of 19% over the past five years. Compare this against other companies (with analyst forecasts) in the industry, which are in aggregate expected to see revenue growth of 8.7% annually. Factoring in the forecast slowdown in growth, it seems obvious that YOC is also expected to grow slower than other industry participants.

Advertisement

The Bottom Line

The most important thing to take away is that there's been no major change in sentiment, with the analysts reconfirming that the business is performing in line with their previous earnings per share estimates. On the negative side, they also downgraded their revenue estimates, and forecasts imply they will perform worse than the wider industry. Yet - earnings are more important to the intrinsic value of the business. Furthermore, the analysts also cut their price targets, suggesting that the latest news has led to greater pessimism about the intrinsic value of the business.

Keeping that in mind, we still think that the longer term trajectory of the business is much more important for investors to consider. At least one analyst has provided forecasts out to 2028, which can be seen for free on our platform here.

You still need to take note of risks, for example - YOC has 2 warning signs we think you should be aware of.

data.title: Trade Alert: Chief Operating Officer of Finland Of Scandic Hotels Group Laura Tarkka Has Sold Stock
data.description:
We'd be surprised if Scandic Hotels Group AB (publ) (STO:SHOT) shareholders haven't noticed that the Chief Operating Officer of Finland, Laura Tarkka, recently sold kr2.8m worth of stock at kr92.31 per share. The eyebrow raising move amounted to a reduction of 46% in their holding.

Scandic Hotels Group Insider Transactions Over The Last Year

The President & CEO, Jens Mathiesen, made the biggest insider sale in the last 12 months. That single transaction was for kr8.4m worth of shares at a price of kr88.91 each. That means that even when the share price was slightly below the current price of kr89.50, an insider wanted to cash in some shares. We generally consider it a negative if insiders have been selling, especially if they did so below the current price, because it implies that they considered a lower price to be reasonable. Please do note, however, that sellers may have a variety of reasons for selling, so we don't know for sure what they think of the stock price. It is worth noting that this sale was only 23% of Jens Mathiesen's holding. Notably Jens Mathiesen was also the biggest buyer, having purchased kr2.5m worth of shares.

In the last twelve months insiders purchased 29.72k shares for kr2.5m. On the other hand they divested 165.64k shares, for kr15m. In total, Scandic Hotels Group insiders sold more than they bought over the last year. You can see the insider transactions (by companies and individuals) over the last year depicted in the chart below. Insider Ownership

Usually, the higher the insider ownership, the more likely it is that insiders will be incentivised to build the company for the long term. Insiders own 1.1% of Scandic Hotels Group shares, worth about kr205m. This level of insider ownership is good but just short of being particularly stand-out. It certainly does suggest a reasonable degree of alignment.

So What Does This Data Suggest About Scandic Hotels Group Insiders?

Unfortunately, there has been more insider selling of Scandic Hotels Group stock, than buying, in the last three months. Zooming out, the longer term picture doesn't give us much comfort. It is good to see high insider ownership, but the insider selling leaves us cautious. While it's good to be aware of what's going on with the insider's ownership and transactions, we make sure to also consider what risks are facing a stock before making any investment decision. data.title: Farmland Partners (FPI) FFO Volatility Tests Bullish Farmland Stability Narrative
data.description:
Farmland Partners (FPI) opened 2026 with Q1 results framed by recent quarterly revenue between US$9.96 million and US$20.68 million and basic EPS ranging from a small loss of about US$0.00 to US$1.22, set against a trailing 12 month EPS of US$0.65. Over the past year, the company has seen revenue move between US$51.79 million and US$57.53 million on a trailing 12 month basis, while basic EPS has ranged from US$0.65 to US$1.45. This provides a clear view of how top line and per share earnings have tracked together. With trailing net profit margins at 55.6% compared with 98.1% a year earlier and shaped by a one off gain of US$18.6 million, these results put profit quality and margin resilience squarely in focus for investors.

See our full analysis for Farmland Partners.

The next step is to set these reported numbers against the most common market narratives around Farmland Partners to see which stories hold up and which are challenged by the margin and earnings profile now on the table.

See what the community is saying about Farmland Partners

Advertisement

FFO of US$18.3m keeps REIT cash metric in focus

  • On a trailing 12 month basis, Funds From Operations sits at US$18.3m, compared with quarterly FFO figures between US$1.3m and US$11.3m over the last six reported quarters. This highlights how REIT style cash earnings differ from the much higher net income that was lifted by the US$18.6m one off gain.
  • Consensus narrative points to stable revenue and margin support from premium farmland, and the FFO data partly backs that by showing multi quarter cash generation. Yet the contrast between US$18.3m of FFO and trailing net income of US$28.8m is a reminder that part of the reported profitability comes from non recurring items, not just ongoing farm rents and related income.
    • The consensus view links long term revenue durability to scarce high quality farmland and robust global food demand. At the same time, the wide range in quarterly FFO, from around US$1.3m to US$11.3m, shows that cash flow can still move around as dispositions and farm performance change period by period.
    • Comments about portfolio rationalisation toward Midwest farmland and capital discipline sit alongside these figures, suggesting that any assessment of long term stability needs to separate this US$18.3m FFO stream from the boost created by the US$18.6m gain included in net income.

Margins at 55.6% with an US$18.6m one off gain

  • Trailing net profit margin is 55.6%, down from 98.1% the prior year, and includes a US$18.6m one off gain that lifts reported profitability relative to what underlying operations alone would show.
  • Bears highlight risks from water driven write downs and crop specific headwinds, and the margin pattern gives that cautious view some grounding because profitability has stepped down from 98.1% to 55.6% while remaining flattered by the US$18.6m gain.
    • Concerns about regulatory driven water issues and around 50% write downs on some specialty crop farms line up with the lower margin, which is now much closer to what you might expect from a REIT that has been selling or impairing assets in challenged regions.
    • Bearish commentary about limited acquisition activity and more reliance on dispositions also ties in with the idea that high recent margins are partly transaction driven, so trailing figures may not fully represent recurring rental income strength.
On these margin figures, skeptics argue the recent profitability owes a lot to asset sales rather than repeatable rent checks, which is exactly the kind of nuance unpacked in the 🐻 Farmland Partners Bear Case.

P/E of 16.2x versus 27.9x industry level

  • FPI trades on a P/E of 16.2x compared with 27.9x for North American Specialized REITs and 22.8x for peers, while the US$10.75 share price sits above the DCF fair value of US$7.92 and below the analyst price target of US$12.50.
  • Consensus narrative talks about long term asset value support from scarce farmland and disciplined capital deployment, and the lower P/E relative to the 27.9x industry figure fits that constructive angle. Yet the share price trading above the US$7.92 DCF fair value and a dividend yield of 3.35% that is not well covered by earnings both serve as reminders that income and valuation signals are mixed rather than one sided.
    • The gap between the US$10.75 price and US$12.50 analyst target aligns with the idea that high quality farmland and portfolio focus can justify a premium to some valuation models, but it also means you are relying on those long term assumptions rather than clear near term earnings coverage of the dividend.
    • The combination of a below industry P/E, a price above DCF fair value, and weaker dividend cover encourages you to weigh whether the farmland story and asset recycling effort are strong enough to support both capital appreciation and that 3.35% yield over time.
If you want to see how these valuation trade offs look when analysts map them into different long term scenarios, the detailed community views around Farmland Partners are unpacked in the 📊 Read the what the Community is saying about Farmland Partners..

data.title: Lincoln Electric (LECO) Margin Improvement Tests Bullish Long Term Narratives
data.description:
Lincoln Electric Holdings (LECO) opened 2026 with investors focused on how its recent run of earnings stacks up, with Q4 2025 revenue at US$1,078.7 million and basic EPS at US$2.48, against trailing 12 month figures through Q4 2025 of US$4.23 billion in revenue and basic EPS of US$9.39, alongside year over year earnings growth of 11.7% cited in the data. Over the last few quarters, revenue has moved from US$983.8 million in Q3 2024 to US$1,088.7 million in Q2 2025 and US$1,078.7 million in Q4 2025, while basic EPS shifted from US$1.78 to a range between roughly US$2.11 and US$2.58, setting up a results season where investors are now weighing an improved net profit margin of 12.3% against expectations for more moderate growth ahead.

See our full analysis for Lincoln Electric Holdings.

With the latest numbers on the table, the next step is to see how this mix of revenue, EPS and margins lines up against the widely shared narratives about Lincoln Electric Holdings's growth potential and risk profile.

See what the community is saying about Lincoln Electric Holdings

Advertisement

Margins Hold Above 12% Net Level

  • Over the last 12 months, Lincoln Electric converted US$4.23b of revenue into US$520.5 million of net income, which works out to a 12.3% net profit margin compared with 11.6% in the prior year cited in the data.
  • What bulls highlight is that this kind of profitability lines up with their view of rising automation and higher margin product mix. At the same time, the current 12.3% net margin sits below the bullish narrative that looks for margins to move toward 14.1% in a few years, so the latest numbers partly support that view while also showing there is still a gap to the bullish scenario.
    • Trailing earnings growth of 11.7% per year over the last year and 13.3% per year over five years fits the bullish idea that the business can keep compounding profits from a solid base.
    • At the same time, the published forecast of about 8.3% annual earnings growth is lower than those trailing growth rates, which is more conservative than the bullish storyline of faster future earnings expansion.

Bulls point to margin strength, automation themes and earnings growth as reasons the story is not finished yet, and you can see how that argument stacks up against the latest figures in the 🐂 Lincoln Electric Holdings Bull Case

Debt Load And Premium P/E Multiple

  • The shares trade around US$265 with a P/E of 27.9x, slightly above the 27.1x peer group and 27.4x US machinery industry averages, while the company also carries a high level of debt flagged as a financial risk in the data.
  • Bears focus on that combination of higher leverage and a premium P/E, arguing that if earnings growth slows toward the roughly 8.3% forecast and revenue growth near 6%, the market might not keep paying a higher multiple for the stock.
    • The fact that those forecast growth rates sit below the broader US market expectations cited in the data gives bears a concrete point when they question how long the 27.9x P/E can be sustained.
    • On top of that, the high debt level means a larger portion of future cash flow will likely need to service obligations, which is a clear contrast with the bearish narrative worry about earnings volatility in cyclical end markets.

Skeptics lean on the premium P/E and high debt to argue the downside case, and you can see how that caution compares with the numbers in the 🐻 Lincoln Electric Holdings Bear Case

DCF Discount Versus Analyst Target

  • Using the current share price of US$265, the stock sits about 15% below the stated DCF fair value of roughly US$311.75, while the analyst consensus price target stands at US$290.44, which is around 9.6% above the current price.
  • Consensus narrative supporters point out that this mix of a DCF estimate above the market price and a consensus target below that DCF suggests analysts are baking in those mid single digit revenue growth forecasts and margin assumptions, even though trailing earnings grew 11.7% and net margin is already at 12.3%.
    • The gap between the US$311.75 DCF fair value and the US$290.44 target underlines how the market view is a bit more restrained than the model that uses current cash flows and discount rates.
    • For an investor, the spread between the current P/E of 27.9x and the industry multiple of 27.4x, alongside that DCF discount, shows how the stock can screen as modestly expensive on earnings while still appearing discounted on a cash flow basis.

Approach 1: nVent Electric Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model projects a company’s future cash flows and then discounts them back to today’s dollars to estimate what the business might be worth now.

For nVent Electric, the model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach, based on cash flows reported and projected in US$. The latest twelve month free cash flow is about $370.4 million. Analysts provide free cash flow estimates out to 2028, with Simply Wall St extrapolating further projections through 2035. By 2028, projected free cash flow is $879.4 million, and the ten year path shows a series of annual figures between roughly $637.7 million and $1.37 billion before discounting.

After discounting these projected cash flows back to today and incorporating a terminal value, the model arrives at an estimated intrinsic value of about $86.00 per share. Compared with the recent share price of US$142.90, the DCF output shows the shares are about 66.2% above this intrinsic estimate. This indicates that nVent Electric may be trading on the expensive side based on this model alone.

Result: OVERVALUED

Approach 2: nVent Electric Price vs Earnings

For profitable companies, the P/E ratio is a useful way to connect what you pay for each share with the earnings that company is currently generating. It gives you a quick sense of how many dollars investors are willing to pay today for one dollar of earnings.

What counts as a “normal” P/E depends heavily on how the market views a company’s growth prospects and risks. Higher expected growth or lower perceived risk can support a higher P/E, while slower growth or higher risk usually calls for a lower one.

nVent Electric currently trades on a P/E of 53.92x. That sits above the Electrical industry average P/E of 36.21x and also above the peer group average of 48.05x. Thus, it makes sense to focus on more recent growth rates, instead. Atomenergoremont AD's EPS has risen over the last 12 months, growing from лв2.40 to лв2.66. data.title: Genovis AB (publ.) Just Missed Earnings - But Analysts Have Updated Their Models
data.description:
Genovis AB (publ.) (STO:GENO) shareholders are probably feeling a little disappointed, since its shares fell 4.4% to kr19.40 in the week after its latest first-quarter results. It looks like a pretty bad result, all things considered. Although revenues of kr34m were in line with analyst predictions, statutory earnings fell badly short, missing estimates by 33% to hit kr0.06 per share. Following the result, the analysts have updated their earnings model, and it would be good to know whether they think there's been a strong change in the company's prospects, or if it's business as usual. So we collected the latest post-earnings statutory consensus estimates to see what could be in store for next year.

Taking into account the latest results, the most recent consensus for Genovis AB (publ.) from three analysts is for revenues of kr147.0m in 2026. If met, it would imply a notable 9.9% increase on its revenue over the past 12 months. Per-share earnings are expected to expand 11% to kr0.41. Before this earnings report, the analysts had been forecasting revenues of kr149.0m and earnings per share (EPS) of kr0.44 in 2026. So it looks like there's been a small decline in overall sentiment after the recent results - there's been no major change to revenue estimates, but the analysts did make a minor downgrade to their earnings per share forecasts.

View our latest analysis for Genovis AB (publ.)

It might be a surprise to learn that the consensus price target was broadly unchanged at kr32.67, with the analysts clearly implying that the forecast decline in earnings is not expected to have much of an impact on valuation. There's another way to think about price targets though, and that's to look at the range of price targets put forward by analysts, because a wide range of estimates could suggest a diverse view on possible outcomes for the business. The most optimistic Genovis AB (publ.) analyst has a price target of kr41.00 per share, while the most pessimistic values it at kr27.00. There are definitely some different views on the stock, but the range of estimates is not wide enough as to imply that the situation is unforecastable, in our view.

Another way we can view these estimates is in the context of the bigger picture, such as how the forecasts stack up against past performance, and whether forecasts are more or less bullish relative to other companies in the industry. It's clear from the latest estimates that Genovis AB (publ.)'s rate of growth is expected to accelerate meaningfully, with the forecast 13% annualised revenue growth to the end of 2026 noticeably faster than its historical growth of 9.1% p.a. over the past five years. By contrast, our data suggests that other companies (with analyst coverage) in a similar industry are forecast to grow their revenue at 6.8% per year. Factoring in the forecast acceleration in revenue, it's pretty clear that Genovis AB (publ.) is expected to grow much faster than its industry.

Advertisement

The Bottom Line

The biggest concern is that the analysts reduced their earnings per share estimates, suggesting business headwinds could lay ahead for Genovis AB (publ.). Happily, there were no major changes to revenue forecasts, with the business still expected to grow faster than the wider industry. There was no real change to the consensus price target, suggesting that the intrinsic value of the business has not undergone any major changes with the latest estimates.
